meta.title: EU Pay Transparency Directive — Member State Tracker
meta.subtitle: Where the 27 Member States Stand
meta.disclaimer: Status and sources verified for ISO week 2026-W18 (28 April 2026). This page does not constitute legal advice.

green.label: Transposed or on-track
green.definition: National law adopted, or final bill confirmed to enter into force on or before 7 June 2026.

amber.label: Draft published
amber.definition: Bill or decree in formal consultation or before parliament; intent clear, deadline at risk.

red.label: No public draft / resisting
red.definition: No formal text published, only initial steps announced — or government has signalled non-compliance.

directivePillars: Pay range disclosed in job ads / before interview
Right of employees to information on pay & criteria
Ban on salary-history questions of candidates
Joint pay assessment if unjustified gap > 5%
Reversed burden of proof in equal-pay litigation

directiveSources.title: European Commission confirms 7 June 2026 deadline holds

SK.name: Slovakia
SK.headline: First past the post — Equal Pay Act adopted 15 April 2026
SK.summary: First Member State across the line. The Equal Pay Act enters into force on 7 June 2026 (the EU deadline). Compliant pay structures are required by 31 July 2026 — barely seven weeks later. Same-sex equal pay claims are in scope (gold-plating). First reports by 7 June 2027 cover only 1 Aug – 31 Dec 2026 (not a full calendar year). Reporting fines €4,000–€8,000, supplemented by broader Labour Inspectorate sanctioning powers.

keyDates.label: First pay-gap report (≥150 staff, covers 1 Aug–31 Dec 2026)

sources.title: Slovakia becomes the first EU Member State to transpose

SK.weeklyCheckSummary: Material update this week — see headline and key dates.

FI.name: Finland
FI.headline: Bill targeted to enter into force 18 May 2026
FI.summary: Government proposal submitted 22 December 2025; consultation closed 9 February 2026; final bill expected before the EU deadline. Tracks Directive thresholds (≥100 staff).

sources.title: Finland Targeting May 18 — Transposition of EU Pay Transparency Directive

IT.headline: Draft legislative decree advancing through parliament
IT.summary: Council of Ministers gave preliminary approval on 5 February 2026; expected to meet the June 2026 deadline. The decree-driven model means rapid finalisation is possible.

MT.headline: Partial text already in force since 27 August 2025
MT.summary: Legal Notice 112 of 2025 brought pay-range disclosure and the salary-history ban into force early. Full alignment with the Directive expected by the deadline.

NL.summary: Draft bill published; transposition will miss the EU deadline but the text is broadly considered the cleanest implementation in the bloc.

CZ.name: Czech Republic
CZ.headline: Public consultation closed 27 April 2026 — minimalist transposition
CZ.summary: Draft published by the Ministry of Labour on 26 March 2026; public consultation closed 27 April 2026 (week 18). Explicitly a 'minimalist transposition'. General entry into force targeted 1 January 2027, but most substantive obligations (Right to Information, pay reporting, joint pay assessments) only apply from 1 January 2028; some provisions phased in to 2031. Czechia does not intend to meet the 7 June 2026 deadline. Notable gold-plating: a dual-report structure (Ministry-prepared headline report + employer-prepared job-group report due 30 April annually) and a mandatory documented remuneration system for every employer.

keyDates.label: Draft presented by Ministry of Labour

sources.title: Analyzing Czechia's Draft Legislation to Transpose the EU Pay Transparency Directive
Czech Republic publishes draft Pay Transparency Act: key deviations and practical issues for employers

CZ.weeklyCheckSummary: Material update this week — see headline and key dates.

PL.name: Poland
PL.headline: Draft December 2025 — tighter than the Directive
PL.summary: Draft published 16 December 2025; introduces shorter procedural deadlines than the Directive (e.g. 30 days instead of 60) and goes beyond the minimum requirements.

BE.name: Belgium
BE.headline: Minister Clarinval confirms full federal transposition by 7 June 2026 (week of 13 April)
BE.summary: Belgium remains a jurisdictional patchwork: the French Community decree has been in force since 1 January 2025; the Flemish public-sector decree (approved March 2026) targets entry into force on 7 June 2026. In a Committee on Social Affairs session in the week of 13 April 2026, federal Minister Clarinval confirmed the intention to fully transpose the Directive into Belgian law by 7 June 2026. The federal private-sector draft is 'expected to circulate shortly'; no public text yet.

keyDates.label: Decree in force (public sector, French Community)
Minister Clarinval confirms full federal transposition

sources.title: Decree partially transposes EU Pay Transparency Directive (Flemish public sector)
Belgium Pay Transparency Reporting Law Guide (Minister Clarinval, week of 13 April 2026)

BE.weeklyCheckSummary: Material update this week — see headline and key dates.

IE.name: Ireland
IE.headline: Government confirms phased rollout — deadline will be missed (22 April 2026)
IE.summary: Two-bill strategy: the Equality (Miscellaneous Provisions) Bill 2024 covers the salary-history ban (Article 5); a standalone Pay Transparency Bill is still in Heads-of-Bill drafting. Government has confirmed Ireland will not be fully compliant by 7 June 2026 and will introduce a phased rollout starting with pre-employment obligations. A Mason Hayes & Curran survey of 500 employers (published 22 April 2026) found over 1 in 5 view the new rules as an 'unnecessary burden' and only 31% expect a positive effect.

keyDates.label: General Scheme of the Equality (Misc. Provisions) Bill

sources.title: One in five employers see pay transparency rule as 'unnecessary burden'
Employers running out of time as changes on pay transparency and retirement loom

ES.name: Spain
ES.headline: Public consultation live — 24 April to 8 May 2026
ES.summary: Public consultation on the draft Royal Decree opened 24 April and closes 8 May 2026 (currently live, week 18). The Decree would broaden workers' rights to information and impose stricter duties on employers in job advertising, pay-setting, career progression and pay audits. Working against the 7 June 2026 deadline; full transposition by that date is not yet certain.

sources.title: Spain moves to strengthen pay transparency and implement EU Gender Pay Transparency Directive

SE.name: Sweden
SE.headline: Resisting — no bill planned (26 March 2026)
SE.summary: On 26 March 2026, the Swedish government called the Directive 'too administratively burdensome'. No bill is planned; the government is seeking renegotiation at EU level.

sources.title: Sweden calls for a renegotiation of the Pay Transparency Directive

EE.name: Estonia
EE.headline: Resisting — partial climbdown after 16 April 2026 statement
EE.summary: On 16 April 2026, Estonia's Economic Affairs Minister stated the government would rather pay European Commission infringement fines than transpose the Directive in its current form. A subsequent clarification confirmed Estonia still intends to prohibit salary history questions and provide applicants with salary range information prior to interview, so the climbdown is partial rather than full. No formal bill on record.

sources.title: Estonia seeks delay to EU equal pay rules despite a wide gender pay gap
